title: How Palin turned on her own party and became governor
summary: In 2006, it would be a supreme irony if the Republicans - the old-line machine Republicans - scored a win against themselves this November, but in some ways they'd only have themselves to blame. Sarah Palin has so far prevailed against all odds, and against her own party's mainstream, which not only is against her but at times lately has seemed to be trying to dismantle her campaign.

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Record salmon catch still stands after 21 years LES ANDERSON: He became known as Mr. Fish after his huge catch. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: May 28, 2006 Last Modified: July 19, 2006 at 04:41 PM Fame was the furthest thing from Les Anderson's mind when he trundled down to the Kenai River with brother-in-law Bud Lofstedt on a fateful May morning 21 years ago. They were angling for big fish, but they didn't have big expectations. Mid-May on the Kenai is relatively slow for king salmon, and typical fish run 20 to 30 pounds -- undersized compared with the 70- and 80-pound monsters of the July late run. Nobody expected a world-record king to come from the Kenai early run. Anderson wasn't after one either. He went fishing that morning, as he had on so many others, because he l…

summary: Barker maintains lead in Seward Salmon Derby Daily News staff Published: August 18, 2006 Last Modified: August 18, 2006 at 02:17 AM The leader board at the 51st Seward Salmon Derby didn't change Thursday, meaning Martha Barker of Sitka is still hoping to take home the first-place prize of $10,000 if she can hold on until noon Sunday. Barker landed her 18.12-pound salmon Wednesday and leads Amanda Wilkens, who caught a 17.76 pounder. 51st annual Seward Silver Salmon Derby 1) Martha Barker, Sitka, 18.12 pounds; 2) Amanda Wilkens, Eagle River, 17.76; 3) Austin Chapman, Seward, 17.15; 4) William Warren, hometown not reported, 17.13; 5) Tim Kain, Santa Rosa, Calif., 17.02. related more SEND US YOUR FISHING SNAPSHOTS Got a photo of yourself or a friend with a nice catch? title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Journalists need to dig deeper for truth in Landis affair CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: August 13, 2006 Last Modified: August 13, 2006 at 01:56 AM Sometimes it's embarrassing to be a journalist, and it's not just because we make mistakes on a regular basis. Mistakes are natural. One could even argue they are evolutionarily essential. Salmon would never populate new streams and rivers if 100 percent of the species returned to the natal stream to spawn. Fortunately some of them get confused and wander, and for that reason, Pacific salmon have spread into many drainages of the Pacific Ocean. Salmon, however, only make one of these mistakes. Then they spawn and die. Journalists get to make mistakes over and over, repeat the mistakes of others, condense information to the point …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Anchorage man sentenced in bear poaching case JAIL: Kwan Su Yi to serve 366 days, forfeit $25,000 boat. By DAN JOLING The Associated Press Published: March 2, 2005 Last Modified: March 2, 2005 at 03:18 AM An Anchorage man convicted of illegally snaring as many as 20 black bears was sentenced Tuesday in federal court to a year and a day in prison. Kwan Su Yi, 33, was not fined but was ordered to forfeit the $25,000 boat used in the poaching, a 22-foot Hewescraft. Prosecutors said Yi and two others snared black bears, removed their gall bladders and plotted to sell them in Korea, where they are used in traditional Asian medicine. They also removed other parts, including legs. "Conduct such as this, in which bears are killed very inhumanely and then largely wasted so that a single part can be…

title: Dolphin's migration for Larry Csonka
summary: A UNALAKLEET -- Larry Csonka is warming his toes by a crackling campfire on the banks of a remote salmon river that spills into the Bering Sea, watching fly fishermen whip baited lines into the current. He twists a cigar in one hand and waves with the other as a boat of Eskimos whooshes past, returning home to a coastal village. "You'll live longer out here," the Miami Dolphins legend says, leaning back in a camp chair as the smoke and the aroma of fresh salmon smoldering on a grill conspire in the cool air. "It's just so tranquil that I think it gets in your DNA. People were doing this for centuries, and now they're sitting around in their houses looking at TV sets.'' There's not a television within miles of where Csonka is lounging, no Internet service to send and receive e-mail. Cell ph…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Mount McKinley's name should declare it is the big one Published: May 16, 2004 Last Modified: May 23, 2004 at 05:23 AM What to call that huge mountain to the north? Many Alaskans call it Denali, which some people say translates as "the Great One." The National Park Service, in its publications, says Denali translates as "the High One." Others have said Denali translates as "the Big One." Is Denali really even the proper Native name for what remains, officially, Mount McKinley? The U.S. Geological Survey's "Dictionary of Alaska Place Names" notes the Tanaina Indian name for McKinley "was reported as Denali, (but) the Tanaina Indian name is 'Doleika' or 'Traleika.' Each of these names is said to mean "the big one" or "the high one." Russians, the first Caucasians to see the mountain, called …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Still top dog Handler who started retriever club in 1959 continues to breed winners By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: August 13, 2006 Last Modified: August 13, 2006 at 03:50 AM TURNAGAIN PASS -- Jag quivered from the tip of his stiff tail to the end of his long, black nose, yet he stayed put. Across the field, a shot rang out and a duck sailed to the ground. Jag watched with the intensity most house dogs show for table scraps. Roy McFall, 81, thin, skin tanned like leather and with fingers twisted from years of hard work, kept a steady hand above Jag's face, not touching the dog but visually commanding him in which direction to concentrate. "Watch," McFall said evenly. "Watch." This repeated as one, then another, duck fell to the ground. Then it was time for Jag to show w…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: LAINE WELCH Russian crab keep pressure on LAINE WELCH FISHERIES Published: July 22, 2006 Last Modified: July 22, 2006 at 03:01 AM KODIAK -- Alaska's Bering Sea crab season will begin with the Aug. 15 start of the golden king crab fishery. Just 14 crabbers own rights to that deepwater fishery under the new system launched last year that divides the catch among boats rather than having the boats compete for the limited catch. Typically only eight boats participate. Golden king crab, which is slightly smaller than red kings and has a more delicate, sweet flavor, is one of Alaska's most stable stocks and for years has maintained a catch of 5.7 million pounds. Unfortunately, all signs point to a continued downward press in world king crab markets. Blame it on the jumbos coming in from the new R…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Nighttime limits on anglers may be grizzly mistake CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: August 6, 2006 Last Modified: August 6, 2006 at 03:31 AM Here we go again. To make the Russian River safe for people, federal officials have decided to give it to the grizzly bears. Well, only at night, they say, as if the bears all had clocks. I understand the thinking. If we give the bears the evening hours alone to feed, they'll eat all those salmon carcasses and then go sleep the day away, safely removed from thousands of anglers. Unless, of course, one of the bears who now considers the river home gets a Cheetos craving and decides to go looking for backpacks with tasty treats inside, as happened last week. Or someone's 8-year-old has to make a potty trip into the bushes and steps on the b…

title: A four-minute Arctic Man
summary: On an only-in-Alaska course, where the X-Games meet the Olympic downhill, Sacha Gros and Tyson Johnson dream of breaking the magical four-minute barrier. Not the four-minute barrier that made British miler Sir Roger Bannister famous but a uniquely Alaska performance standard for man and machine. Gros and Johnson are the record-holding men of the GCI Arctic Man Ski & Sno Go Classic, and on Friday they will try to take a snowmachine-towed skier to places no Arctic Man has gone before. The challenge? Hurtle 1,700 feet and almost two miles down off the top of a 5,800-foot mountain at speeds approaching 80 mph. Slow to 50 mph or thereabouts for a right hand turn and the meeting with a speeding snowmachine dragging a tow rope. Latch onto the rope without losing speed, and then hang on for a 2 ¼-…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Southcentral Alaska teeming with fish GRAB YOUR LURES: From Kenai to the Mat-Su Borough, salmon waiting to be caught. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: August 10, 2006 Last Modified: August 10, 2006 at 03:03 AM The fishy bounty of 2006 makes this a joyfully difficult time to be an Alaska angler. Do you head south from Anchorage to intercept the red salmon pouring into the Kenai River almost a million strong? Or do you point the motorhome north to the streams along the George Parks Highway in search of the silver salmon that stormed through Cook Inlet while commercial fishermen were beached by what seemed like a disastrous Kenai River red run early on? Or maybe you want to go east to Valdez where the charter halibut fleet is now making its mark with big flatfish. A Valdez Hali…
